Going back to Dec. 31, 1999, you would not have known that tough times were soon in order, with the S&P 500 about to post declines for the next three consecutive years, followed later by the Global Financial Crisis of 2008.

Much later would be the COVID-19-related downturn in 2020, the losses in 2022, and most recently, the tariff-related tumult in early April 2025. Over long horizons, most stocks underperformed the market,5 and we know that most active managers underperformed with them, perhaps because historically, holding onto volatile stocks through painful periods of underperformance has required courage when one’s natural instinct is to sell.

1 The Astonishing Bull Market Will End One Day. Are You Ready? - NY Times

2 The stocks recommended can, but do not have to be a member of the S&P 500 at the time of this exercise.

3 The prescience of the forecaster who recommended Stock 1 is admirable, but we know that stock market forecasting is notoriously difficult, as any reader of our SPIVA® Scorecards will recognize.

4 Nvidia and Amazon joined the S&P 500 in November 2001 and November 2005, respectively.

5 Bessembinder, Hendrik. “Which U.S. Stocks Generated the Highest Long-Term Returns,” Nov. 11, 2024.

Disclosure: Copyright © 2025 S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC, a division of S&P Global. All rights reserved. This material is reproduced with the prior written consent of S&P DJI. For more information on S&P DJI, please visit www.spdji.com. For full terms of use and disclosures, please visit www.spdji.com/terms-of-use.

data.title: CAE: Civil Aviation Softness Masks A Defense Upswing And A Deep Value Entry
data.description:
CAE (CAE, CAE:CA), the global leader in flight training and simulation, has bright growth prospects on mid-to-long-term demand drivers in the commercial aviation and defense industries. However, since my last report, the stock has remained flat while the S&P 500 rose 6.1%. I believe the underperformance relative to the company’s execution and growth prospects does provide an attractive entry point. However, given that the company does not quite perform in line with its prospects, keeping a close eye on earnings and industry dynamics is important. In this report, I discuss the Q2 2026 earnings, the growth drivers for the company, and an update to my price target.

Dollar figures in this report are Canadian dollars, unless mentioned otherwise.

CAE Q2 Earnings Hindered By Civil Aviation Softness

CAE’s second quarter sales rose 9% to $1.24 billion, while gross margins expanded modestly by 20 basis points to 25.8%. Operating income surged 31% to $155.3 million. Excluding $30.9 million in restructuring charges in the comparable period last year, operating income grew 4%, indicating margins falling from 13.1% to 12.6%, driven by higher selling, general, and administrative expenses.

Civil Aviation revenues rose 5% to $670 million, while adjusted operating income declined 6%. The increase in sales was driven by the results of SIMCOM being added to the consolidated results as CAE increased its ownership last year and higher second-hand simulator sales. Utilization of flight simulators dropped from 70% to 64%, while the training capacity and thus fixed cost rose. Furthermore, there were six fewer full-motion flight simulator deliveries to customers.

Gross profit grew 5% to $198.6 million, indicating stability in the margins. Adjusted income declined, driven by lower demand for business and commercial services, while depreciation and amortization have increased in combination with higher SG&A.

For Civil Aviation, the long story short is that the company had lower margin growth driven by second-hand flight simulator sales, and demand for training solutions is currently soft. The company ended the quarter with an $8.5 billion backlog compared to $6.7 billion a year ago.

In the defense segment, CAE has restructured its business and started focusing on bigger and higher-margin defense contracts. Previously the company had legacy contracts in the pipeline that were not profitable. Those contracts are not less impactful, as some have been completed, and others have been derisked, while new backlog flowing into the revenues comes with a higher margin. Revenues rose 8% to $76.7 million, driven by higher activity on existing programs and ramp up of new programs in North America. Gross profit increased 19% to $18.9 million, driven by the better contract mix. Adjusted operating income grew 41% to $13.5 million on better operating leverage.

Market Dynamics Unchanged: Civil Aviation Softness, Defense Transition

The market dynamics remain unchanged compared to my assessment in the prior quarter. Civil Aviation training demand remains soft with a book-to-bill ratio of 0.88x and 1.08x when considering backlog adjustments. So, it is definitely a soft spot, but in the long term, demand is firmly supported by a growing global airplane fleet as well as training required for new pilots.

The defense business has been pressured by legacy contracts for quite some time. These contracts were fixed-price contracts agreed on before the pandemic, and after the pandemic, cost estimates rose partially also due to inflation, and that pressured the defense business. Currently, three legacy contracts remain to be completed, but we are already seeing better backlog flowing through the system, resulting in a substantial improvement in sales and margins. The adjusted backlog is $11.6 billion compared to $11.4 billion a year ago. The order intake was $555.8 million, bringing the book-to-bill ratio to roughly 1x.

CAE Stock Remains A "Buy"

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

Figures below are in USD.

For CAE, I have a $32.30 target in the base case and $37.36 in the more bullish scenario. EBITDA estimates for 2026-2028 declined by 1.8% with a 2.4% downward revision this year, 1% next year, and 0.4% in the year after. CARG has remained more or less stable at 7.8%. For free cash flow, estimates have decreased by 0.5%, with a 3% decline this year, 0.3% next year, but an upward revision of 1.1% by FY28. CAGR has improved modestly from 8.9% to 9.3%. So, I am maintaining my "Buy" rating. My prior base case and more bullish targets were $33.37 and $37.93, so there is a downward revision on the price targets, but at 18%-36%, the upside remains compelling.

The key metrics show that EBITDA is expected to rise in the years ahead, but on stable margins, the increase from FY25 to FY26 is expected to be modest. The same holds for free cash flow, with a weaker free cash flow conversion expected this year, but this is expected to grow in the years ahead as CapEx as a percentage of operating cash flow will come down. We note that the company should be able to bring leverage down substantially, showing a strong balance sheet.

Conclusion: CAE Remains Attractive For The Long-Term

CAE’s results do show softness in civil aviation and robust growth in defense. That is seemingly something that is expected throughout the year, with little margin growth expected this year. However, the investment case remains compelling, as CAE is undervalued against aerospace and defense peers, and demand for its products and services is supported by longer-term trends.

data.title: Universal Technical Institute: Concorde Deregulation Signals New Upside
data.description:
The market still treats Universal Technical Institute (UTI) as a traditional vocational school specializing in mechanics and diesel, ignoring the transformation it has made through its acquisitions, such as Concorde Career Colleges, which represents a pivotal point in our thesis. During the Q3 call, the company revealed that it had satisfied the DOE requirements for lifting the growth restrictions imposed on Concorde. Therefore, since the market had lowered its growth expectations for this segment because of those regulatory restrictions, it should reassess the stock's valuation after lifting them.

Concorde is an educational institution that focuses on training specialists in the healthcare sector, which grows by 20% compared to 12% in the traditional segment. With the recent price decline due to profit-taking, the stock represents an excellent entry point.

The Shift To a Platform

Jerome Grant, the CEO of UTI, launched a plan known as North Star, which stands on three fundamentals: Growth, Diversification, and Optimization, with a special focus on diversification to reduce reliance on one segment (Auto & Diesel). The company executed a series of acquisitions in several fields, the first was in 2021 when it acquired MIAT College of Technology, which specializes in Airframes & Powerplant for $26 million. Then, in 2022, it was a turning point when it acquired Concorde Career Colleges for $50 million. Concorde operates in the healthcare sector, including nursing and dentistry etc. This deal added revenues that reached $180 million at that time. Importantly, Grant's vision indicates the goal is to integrate these entities with their systems into each other to build a unified platform that addresses the critical shortage of skilled labor, which will reduce costs as spending will be concentrated on a unified entity with higher efficiency instead of three separate ones. Consequently, increasing profit margins over time.

The Regulatory Environment

The vocational education sector has been living under a strict Gainful Employment rule. The goal of it is to ensure that educational programs do not burden graduates with debts they can't pay, so it stipulates that annual loan installments for graduates must be less than 8% of their total annual income, and also demands that graduate income must exceed the average income of a high school graduate in the same state.

Consequently, some believe that these rules will reduce the number of new students due to their inability to meet the requirements for federal loans. However, UTI programs are different as the school is originally designed to solve the severe labor shortage in fields such as mechanics, aviation, and nursing, where they offer well-paying jobs that can easily pass GE requirements. The other matter is that when the company acquired Concorde in 2022, it had restrictions subject to the Provisional Program Participation Agreement (PPA) that prevented it from expanding into new programs. However, in Q3, the management mentioned that DOE lifted these restrictions, which limited its growth. As a result, it can now expand into new programs and branches, accelerating its growth by much more than 20%.

Competition

UTI owns a competitive advantage represented in its strong relationships with major manufacturers. It has specialized training partnerships and programs with companies like Ford Motor Company (F), Bayerische Motoren Werke Aktiengesellschaft (OTC:BMWKY), and Toyota Motor Corporation (TM). These partnerships ensure that its curriculum is continuously updated. It also provides a factory-certified certificate to the graduate, giving them priority in employment with these companies, while its competitors, like Lincoln Educational Services Corporation (LINC), do not own this depth of exclusive partnerships.

In addition, the Concorde acquisition is considered a new moat for the company in the healthcare sector. While LINC tries to build nursing programs (LPN) from scratch, Concorde is a well-known brand that includes 17 campuses and 7,500 students; thus, UTI is years ahead of its competitors in this area. Furthermore, the acquisition of MIAT College of Technology allowed the company to apply in specialized programs that community colleges cannot easily access due to strict regulatory approvals, such as Airframe and Powerplant, which requires FAA approval.

Financials

UTI has a strong record exceeding analysts' expectations. In Q3, UTI's traditional segment revenues reached $131.5 million while Concorde segment generated $72.8 million for a total of $204.3 million, noting that Concorde, which represents 36% of the company's revenues, grew by 20.7% vs 12.2% for UTI's traditional segment. This means that it grows at a faster pace, indicating greater demand for healthcare programs. As for adjusted EBITDA, it reached $25.3 million with an increase of 37.3% YoY, meaning that every new dollar from revenues generates a higher profit than before. Every quarter, the revenue mix shifts more towards Concorde, which is faster and has higher profitability than the traditional segment, contributing to increasing the total growth rate and profitability for the company. In general, UTI is transforming into a fully vocational education company with a leadership in the healthcare segment.

Guidance

The company expects the strong performance to continue during Q4, driven by Concorde after the DOE lifted the regulatory restrictions and continued improvement in operational performance in the traditional segment. Regarding the full year, the management expects revenues between $830 and $835 million, representing +15% growth. It also expects +$124 million for the adjusted EBITDA. The removal of restrictions on Concorde will enable it to accelerate geographic expansion and launch new programs in 2026, making revenues lean more towards the high-demand/growth healthcare segment, which will translate into higher revenues and profits during the coming period.

Valuation

According to the company's guidance, it expects adjusted EBITDA of $124 million for 2025. UTI has an EV/EBITDA of approximately 12x. However, the multiple of its closest competitor, LINC, is 20x. I will assume the average of 16x to put a margin of safety. With that in mind and after adding net cash of $40 million, the equity value comes to $2024 million, or $37 per share (shares outstanding at 54.4 million).

The price target represents a potential increase of more than 20% from the current trading levels $30.

Risks

No investment is without risk, so the first risk relates to excessive optimism regarding the new regulations for Gainful Employment. The regulations may be more stringent than anticipated, and the company may fail to pass some of its graduate income tests. New students may find it difficult to obtain federal loans, which will inevitably impact revenue. However, the high demand from companies for UTI graduates means there is a severe labor shortage, and therefore, the salaries offered will be sufficient to pass the tests.

After acquiring two large companies (Concorde and MIAT), if UTI fails to integrate their systems into a single platform, the company will have to manage each company's system separately. This will increase operating expenses instead of reducing them. Furthermore, a failed integration could lead to operational conflicts and delays in decision-making, which will damage the brand's reputation and slow down the enrollment of new students.

Some bears believe the company is experiencing a slowdown in growth, citing a 2.8% increase in new student enrollment in the third quarter. However, a closer look reveals that it's the traditional UTI segment that has slowed growth, while the more important revenue-generating metric, average active student growth, has been 12.7%, reaching 18.8% for the Concorde segment.

Conclusion

UTI is no longer just a mechanical school; it's on its way to becoming a diverse workforce platform, driven by Concorde, after it gets released from restrictions. In my opinion, the recent pullback after the stock's significant surge is due to profit-taking, making it a buying opportunity for long-term investors. However, investors should watch any management comments on Concorde's expansion plan following the deregulation, as well as monitor the number of new students, which is expected to accelerate. Also, be sure to follow any updates from the company or DOE regarding UTI's debt-to-Earnings tests (Gainful Employment), which is expected to be positive.
